But a heavily oversubscribed, richly priced OFS also carries the usual caution: much of the optimism may already be baked into the price, so early gains can be volatile.
stories.why_now: The listing rides a multi-year surge in Indian mutual-fund investing — record monthly SIP inflows have swelled the assets these firms manage — making this a well-timed moment for SBI and Amundi to sell a slice at a strong valuation, and for a market hungry for large, brand-name financial IPOs to lap it up.
stories.watch_next: The July 21 listing — whether the shares open near the ~16% grey-market premium or fade — and the final allotment on July 17.

key_terms.definition: The first time a company sells its shares to the public and lists on a stock exchange, letting anyone buy in and setting a market price.
An IPO in which existing shareholders sell their own shares, so the proceeds go to them — not into the company, which raises no fresh capital.

'Oversubscribed 42x' means investors asked for 42 times the shares on offer. QIBs (qualified institutional buyers) are big professional investors like banks, insurers and foreign funds; their portion here drew ~140x demand.
A firm that runs mutual funds — pooling investors' money and managing it for a fee. SBI Funds Management is India's largest.

stories.the_lesson: How a share sale is structured tells you where the money flows. In an 'offer for sale', the cash goes to the owners cashing out, not to the company — so a wildly oversubscribed IPO can be a great deal for the sellers while the business itself gets nothing new to grow with. And when institutions bid far more eagerly than retail investors, it's a clue about which theme — here, Indians pouring savings into mutual funds — the smart money is betting on.
stories.market_link: A direct read-through for State Bank of India (SBIN), the selling parent, and a sentiment gauge for listed asset managers and the broader 'financialisation of savings' trade; watch SBI Funds Management's July 21 debut on the NSE/BSE.

stories.title: Wipro's profit goes flat and its outlook stays gloomy — India's IT giant signals the sector's slump isn't over as AI reshapes the business
key_points: Q1 net profit ₹3,352 crore — flat on last year, down 4.7% on the quarter
Just missed analysts' forecast of about ₹3,460 crore
Guides for flat-to-weak IT-services revenue next quarter
Declares a ₹2-per-share interim dividend; margins slip to 16%
stories.background: Wipro is one of India's 'Big Four' IT-services companies — alongside TCS, Infosys and HCLTech — the firms that write software and run the back-office technology for banks, retailers and manufacturers around the world, mostly in the US and Europe. This industry is a pillar of India's economy: it earns huge amounts of foreign currency, employs millions of engineers, and its quarterly results are treated as a health check on both global corporate spending and Indian markets. For the past two years the sector has been in a slump — nervous Western clients, worried about high interest rates and a shaky economy, have frozen or trimmed their tech budgets — and now a fresh threat looms: generative AI, which can automate some of the very coding and support work these firms sell, forcing them to reinvent what they offer.
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, Wipro reported April-June (its fiscal first quarter) net profit of ₹3,352 crore — flat versus a year earlier and down 4.7% from the previous quarter, just short of the roughly ₹3,460 crore analysts expected. Revenue rose 10.6% from a year ago to ₹24,480 crore, but its core IT-services business — the number investors watch most — barely grew, up about 1% on the year and actually down 1.4% quarter-on-quarter. Operating margin slipped 130 basis points (1.3 percentage points) to 16%, squeezed by employee pay rises and spending on new AI capabilities; the finance chief admitted the company's target margin of 17-18% is out of reach for the next few quarters. Crucially, Wipro guided for muted revenue in the current quarter, and declared an interim dividend of ₹2 per share.
stories.why_it_matters: As one of the first big Indian IT firms to report each season, Wipro's numbers set the tone for the whole sector and often move the Nifty IT index. Flat profit plus a cautious forecast tells investors the long-hoped-for recovery in global tech spending still hasn't arrived — and that the margin squeeze from paying staff more while investing in AI is real. It's an early read on whether India's showcase export industry can turn artificial intelligence from a threat into a new source of growth, or whether it keeps eating into the old business faster than new work replaces it.
stories.ripple_effects: A weak Wipro print and soft guidance can drag on peers TCS, Infosys and HCLTech and on the broader Nifty IT index, since investors read one company's caution as a signal for the group. For the hundreds of thousands of engineers these firms employ, a prolonged slump means fewer hires, smaller raises and more pressure to reskill for AI work. Because the sector is India's biggest source of services export earnings, a durable slowdown also matters for the rupee and the country's trade balance. And it feeds a bigger debate: whether AI will ultimately shrink the headcount-heavy 'bodies on projects' model that built India's $250bn-plus IT industry.
stories.why_now: The report lands at the start of the July earnings season, when India's IT majors report one after another, so it's the market's first hard data point on how the sector fared last quarter. It arrives against a backdrop of still-cautious Western clients and mounting questions about how fast generative AI will reshape — and possibly shrink — the traditional IT-outsourcing business.
stories.watch_next: The upcoming results and guidance from TCS, Infosys and HCLTech, which will confirm whether Wipro's muted outlook reflects a sector-wide slump or a company-specific stumble.

key_terms.definition: Money earned from building and running software and technology systems for clients — the core business of firms like Wipro, and the figure investors watch most closely.
The share of revenue left as profit after day-to-day running costs; a falling margin means the business is keeping less of each rupee it earns.
One-hundredth of a percentage point. Wipro's margin fell 130 basis points — that is, by 1.3 percentage points.
A company's own forecast for its coming performance; 'muted guidance' means management expects little or no growth ahead.

stories.the_lesson: A single company's results are a window onto a whole industry — and onto a bigger shift. Wipro's flat profit and cautious tone show how a business built on selling human hours gets squeezed twice over: once when clients cut budgets, and again when a new technology like AI threatens to do some of that work itself. The firms that thrive will be the ones that sell the AI rather than get replaced by it.
stories.market_link: Directly moves Wipro (WIPRO) shares and the Nifty IT index; a read-through for TCS, Infosys and HCLTech ahead of their results, and a signpost for the rupee given IT's role as India's top services exporter.

stories.title: India draws up a $51bn 'make it at home' hit-list — Modi orders a push to replace imports and shield the economy from oil shocks, a weak rupee and China's grip on supplies
key_points: Govt flags $51bn of 'critical' imports to be made in India instead
About 100 items — from textiles to solar panels — get immediate action
Analysis: up to $398bn of India's $775bn imports could be localised
Subsidies and incentives planned; aim is to ease pressure on the rupee
stories.background: India buys far more from the world than it sells — it imported about $775 billion of goods in the year to March 2026 — and that gap has to be paid for in foreign currency, mostly US dollars. When imports surge (say, when oil prices spike), India needs more dollars, which pushes the rupee down and makes everything imported dearer. India has long wanted to make more of what it uses at home — the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' (self-reliant India) drive — and has offered 'PLI' schemes (production-linked incentives: cash rewards paid to companies for making more here). This new plan widens that effort into a systematic hunt for imports the country could realistically replace with local production.
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, reports (led by Reuters and Bloomberg) said Prime Minister Narendra Modi's office has ordered key ministries to comb through India's import bill and identify goods the country is heavily dependent on but could make at home. Officials have flagged about $51 billion of 'critical' imports — inputs used across industries from textiles to solar panels — as the priority pool, with roughly 100 specific items lined up for immediate action. A broader internal analysis suggested that up to $398 billion of India's total imports could, over time, potentially be replaced by domestic manufacturing. To make that happen, the government is weighing subsidies and other incentives to nudge companies to produce these goods locally.
stories.why_it_matters: This is India trying to 'shock-proof' its economy at a tense moment: a US-Iran war is pushing up oil prices, the rupee has slid past 96 to the dollar, and China — a huge supplier of India's industrial inputs — has been curbing exports of critical materials like rare earths. Cutting reliance on imports would ease the drain of dollars, steady the currency, and reduce the risk that a foreign crisis or a rival's export ban can throttle Indian factories. It's a strategic bet that making more at home buys both economic stability and geopolitical insurance.
stories.ripple_effects: If it works, Indian manufacturers of solar equipment, textiles, electronics parts, chemicals and machinery could see fresh demand and government cash — a potential boost for 'Make in India' firms and jobs. But import substitution has a catch: shielding local producers can mean higher prices or lower quality for buyers if domestic factories aren't yet competitive, and it can invite trade friction if partners feel shut out. Success also hinges on things India can't fix overnight — reliable power, skilled workers, and access to the very raw materials (like rare earths) that China controls. Watch for new PLI-style schemes, tariff tweaks, and sector-specific targets in the coming months.
stories.why_now: The push is surfacing now because several pressures have stacked up at once: the Iran conflict has spiked oil (India's single biggest import), the rupee is under strain, and China's restrictions on critical minerals have exposed how vulnerable Indian supply chains are. Modi's office is responding by trying to identify and shrink those dependencies before the next external shock hits.
stories.watch_next: Which sectors and specific items the government names first, and whether it rolls out new subsidy or PLI-style incentive schemes to back the import-substitution drive.

key_terms.definition: A strategy of making goods at home instead of buying them from abroad, to cut reliance on foreign suppliers and save foreign currency.
A government programme that pays companies cash rewards tied to how much they produce (and sell) domestically, to encourage manufacturing in India.
A group of metals essential for magnets, electronics and clean-energy gear; China dominates their supply and has restricted exports, exposing importers' vulnerability.
Hindi for 'self-reliant India' — the government's long-running campaign to make more of what the country consumes at home.

stories.the_lesson: A big import bill isn't just an economic number — it's a vulnerability. Every essential thing a country must buy from abroad is a lever someone else can pull, through prices, a weak currency, or an export ban. That's why nations under pressure try to make more of their own critical goods: self-reliance is as much about security as about savings.
stories.market_link: Positive read-through for domestic manufacturers in solar, electronics, chemicals and capital goods that could win incentives; a supportive backdrop for the rupee if import demand eases, and a signal to watch new PLI and tariff measures.

stories.title: Files from India's biggest nuclear plant, Kudankulam, turn up on the dark web — a contractor admits a 'partial breach' as CERT-In investigates
key_points: ~19,000 files (14.3 GB) tied to the Kudankulam nuclear plant leaked on the dark web
Hacker group 'World Lix' claims it; files online since June 11
Reliance Group, a plant contractor, admits a 'partial breach' on a Yotta-hosted server
India's cyber agency CERT-In is investigating; a minister played down the risk
stories.background: The Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant (KKNPP), on the coast of Tamil Nadu in south India, is the country's largest nuclear power station, built with Russian help and run by the state-owned Nuclear Power Corporation of India (NPCIL). Nuclear plants are treated as 'critical infrastructure' — facilities so vital that an attack on them could endanger public safety — so their technical details are meant to be closely guarded. This isn't Kudankulam's first scare: back in 2019 the plant's administrative network was hit by malware later linked to North Korean hackers, a reminder that even air-gapped nuclear sites are targets. Today's incident involves not the reactor controls themselves but a trove of documents held by one of the plant's private contractors.
stories.what_happened: In mid-July 2026, Reuters reported that nearly 19,000 files totalling about 14.3 gigabytes and tied to Kudankulam had been sitting on the dark web — the hidden part of the internet reachable only with special software — since June 11, uploaded by a hacker group calling itself 'World Lix'. Reliance Group, the conglomerate controlled by businessman Anil Ambani and a contractor on the project, confirmed a 'partial breach' of its data on a server run by third-party Indian data-centre provider Yotta. Reuters said it reviewed the documents — which are dated from 2016 to mid-2025 and purportedly include blueprints, supplier details, meeting and inspection records, equipment reviews and insurance policies — but could not verify their authenticity. NPCIL is in touch with Reliance, India's Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) is investigating, and Union Science Minister Jitendra Singh publicly downplayed the risk.
stories.why_it_matters: Even if no reactor was hacked, leaking blueprints, supplier lists and inspection records of a nuclear plant hands potential attackers a map of the site's weak points — which is why a nuclear-security expert quoted by Reuters called the risk 'serious'. It also exposes a growing blind spot: the biggest danger to critical infrastructure often isn't the guarded facility itself but the web of private contractors and cloud providers around it, whose servers are softer targets. For India, racing to expand both its nuclear fleet and its digital economy, it's an uncomfortable test of how well it protects its most sensitive data.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect tighter scrutiny of the contractors and cloud vendors that handle sensitive government and infrastructure data, and pressure on firms like Reliance and Yotta to prove their security. It fuels the wider debate over India's data-protection and cybersecurity rules, and over how much of the state's critical work should sit on third-party servers. Diplomatically, any hint of a foreign hand — as in 2019 — could sharpen India's cyber-defence posture. And for ordinary citizens, it's a reminder that a breach at a distant contractor can touch something as consequential as a nuclear plant.
stories.why_now: The files had quietly been online since June 11, but the story broke this week after Reuters investigated the dark-web cache and Reliance confirmed the breach — turning a hidden leak into a public national-security question just as India is pushing an ambitious nuclear-expansion drive.
stories.watch_next: The findings of the CERT-In investigation — whether it identifies who 'World Lix' is, how the contractor's server was breached, and whether any genuinely sensitive reactor data was exposed.

key_terms.definition: Systems so essential — power plants, water, telecom, nuclear sites — that damaging or disrupting them could threaten public safety or national security, so they get special protection.
A hidden layer of the internet, reachable only with special anonymising software, where stolen data and illegal goods are often traded out of easy public view.
The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team — the government's main cybersecurity agency, which responds to and investigates hacking incidents and data breaches.
The Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited — the state-owned company that builds and operates India's nuclear power plants, including Kudankulam.

stories.the_lesson: A system is only as secure as its weakest link — and for guarded facilities like nuclear plants, that link is increasingly the outside contractors and cloud servers that hold their data, not the fortified site itself. Cybersecurity has to cover the whole supply chain of vendors, or the front door stays locked while the back door swings open.
stories.market_link: Not a major market mover, but it puts a spotlight on Anil Ambani's Reliance Group entities named as a contractor, on data-centre provider Yotta, and more broadly on Indian cybersecurity and IT-services firms that stand to gain as critical-infrastructure security spending rises.

stories.title: Deadly crush at Puri's Rath Yatra again: at least one devotee dead and 100+ hospitalised in a crowd surge — a year after a stampede here killed three
key_points: A crowd surge hit the Rath Yatra at Puri's Singhadwara (Lion's Gate) area
At least one devotee died — reports of the toll vary; 100+ hospitalised
Nearly 13,000 police were deployed, yet the crush still happened
Comes a year after a June 2025 stampede at the same festival killed three
stories.background: The Rath Yatra ('chariot festival') in Puri, Odisha, is one of Hinduism's biggest annual events: the deities Lord Jagannath, his brother Balabhadra and sister Subhadra are pulled through the streets on three towering wooden chariots by hundreds of thousands of devotees, who believe helping to haul the ropes brings blessings. The sheer density of the crowd — packed into narrow lanes around the Jagannath Temple's Singhadwara, or 'Lion's Gate' — makes crowd control extremely hard. This is a recurring danger: just last year, on June 29, 2025, a stampede during the same festival killed three people and injured dozens, after which Odisha's chief minister called the negligence 'unpardonable.'
stories.what_happened: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, a stampede-like crowd surge broke out among devotees near the Singhadwara/Marichikund area as the chariots were being readied and pulled. At least one devotee died — reported to be from suffocation in the crush — while news outlets carried varying figures, with some reporting additional deaths that were not immediately confirmed. Over 100 people (some reports said up to around 200) were taken to hospital with suffocation, fainting and injuries, worsened by heavy rain and the packed conditions. Authorities said nearly 13,000 police personnel, including senior officers, had been deployed to manage the crowds — yet the surge still occurred. The death toll and exact cause were still being established as of this report.
stories.why_it_matters: A second fatal crush at the very same festival in consecutive years turns a tragic accident into a pattern — and a pointed question about whether crowd-safety lessons from 2025 were actually put into practice. It is a stark reminder that at mass religious gatherings, the crowd itself becomes the hazard: once bodies are packed tightly enough, a small stumble or a collapsed barricade can trigger a deadly domino effect that no amount of faith or policing can instantly stop.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect immediate political fallout in Odisha — condolences, compensation for victims' families, an official inquiry, and opposition attacks over preparedness — echoing the response after the 2025 deaths. The incident will renew a nationwide debate about crowd management at India's giant religious festivals (from the Kumbh Mela to temple town events), where similar crushes recur. Temple and district administrations nationwide may face pressure to adopt stricter crowd-flow design, capacity limits, better barricading and real-time monitoring. For the millions who travel to Puri, it also raises hard questions about balancing open access to a sacred ritual with basic safety.
stories.why_now: The surge happened at the peak moment of the festival, when the largest crowds gather to pull the chariots after the traditional rituals — the same high-density window that has repeatedly turned dangerous. Heavy monsoon rain and the crush of devotees at a narrow chokepoint tipped an already-packed crowd into a deadly surge.
stories.watch_next: The confirmed death toll and the findings of any official inquiry into how the crush happened despite the large police deployment.

key_terms.definition: The annual 'chariot festival' in Puri, Odisha, where huge crowds pull the deities Jagannath, Balabhadra and Subhadra through the streets on giant wooden chariots.
The main eastern entrance of Puri's Jagannath Temple, a natural chokepoint where the festival's densest crowds gather.
When a densely packed crowd surges or collapses, people can be trampled or suffocated; deaths often come from compression, not trampling — you can't breathe when the crowd squeezes too tightly.

stories.the_lesson: At mass gatherings the crowd itself is the risk: past a certain density, a single stumble or a broken barricade can cascade into a fatal crush, and packing more police in doesn't help if the space and the flow of people aren't designed for the numbers. Preventing disasters is about managing density and exits in advance, not reacting once the surge has begun.

stories.title: India's fiercest federal fight is back: the Modi government revives the 'delimitation' bill that could shift power from the south to the north — and Kharge demands an all-party meeting
key_points: Govt to revive the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill in the July 20 Monsoon session
Bill ties 33% women's reservation to redrawing Lok Sabha seats by population
Southern states fear losing seats for having controlled their populations
It lost in April; NDA is now ~6 seats short of the two-thirds it needs
stories.background: India's Lok Sabha (the directly elected lower house of Parliament) has 543 seats, each roughly meant to represent an equal number of people. But the number and boundaries of those seats — a process called 'delimitation' — have been frozen since the 1970s to avoid punishing states that slowed their population growth. Meanwhile, a 2023 law promised to reserve one-third of all seats for women, but tied that promise to a fresh delimitation based on a new census. The result is a knot: you can't fully deliver women's reservation without redrawing the seats, and redrawing the seats reopens a decades-old regional wound. In April 2026 the government's attempt to untie that knot — the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill — was defeated on the floor of the Lok Sabha.
stories.what_happened: The Modi government is preparing to bring the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill, 2026 back before Parliament when the Monsoon session opens on July 20, with 'revised proposals' after the original was defeated on April 17 (298 votes for, 230 against — well short of the two-thirds majority a constitutional change requires). The bill would delink women's reservation from the census timeline and allow delimitation using 2011 census data, potentially expanding the Lok Sabha from 543 seats toward as many as 848. On July 16, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge wrote to Prime Minister Modi demanding an all-party meeting to discuss the revised plan; the government has separately called an all-party meeting for July 19. The ruling NDA alliance is estimated to be around six seats short of the numbers it needs and is courting outside parties — with NCP (Sharad Pawar) leader Supriya Sule hinting her party might support the bill under certain conditions.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a fight over the most basic currency of a democracy — how many seats, and therefore how much national power, each region gets. Southern and some north-eastern states successfully slowed their population growth over decades; a headcount-based redraw would hand more seats to the faster-growing north (Uttar Pradesh, Bihar), effectively reducing the south's relative voice in Parliament. So a technical-sounding 'delimitation' bill actually decides whether the states that did what the country asked — control population — get penalised for it.
stories.ripple_effects: If it passes, the political map tilts north: projections suggest that even with an expanded house, states like Tamil Nadu and Kerala could see their share of seats shrink while Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Rajasthan gain — reshaping which regions parties must win to rule India. It could inflame the north-south divide, energising regional parties in the south to campaign against 'northern domination', and strain Centre-state relations over funding and language. If it fails again, women's reservation stays stuck in limbo, and the government spends political capital with nothing to show. Either way, it sets the tone for the whole Monsoon session and the run-up to future elections.
stories.why_now: The Monsoon session of Parliament begins July 20, giving the government its next window to try again after April's defeat; with the numbers still tight, both sides are manoeuvring this week — the government calling an all-party meeting for July 19 and the Opposition (via Kharge's July 16 letter) demanding one of its own and vowing to stay united against the bill.
stories.watch_next: The July 19 all-party meeting and whether the government tables the revised bill on July 20 — and if it does, whether fence-sitters like the NCP (SP) give it the extra votes needed for a two-thirds majority.

key_terms.definition: The redrawing of electoral constituency boundaries and the number of seats each state gets, usually based on the latest population count.
A proposed change to India's constitution linking women's reservation to a fresh delimitation; changing the constitution needs a two-thirds majority in Parliament, not a simple one.
The idea that states which successfully slowed population growth would lose parliamentary seats under a population-based redraw — being 'punished' for good policy.
A 2023 law setting aside one-third of Lok Sabha and state-assembly seats for women, but only after a new delimitation — which is why the two issues are now tangled together.

stories.the_lesson: How you count people decides who holds power. Tying representation to raw population sounds fair, but it can quietly punish regions that did what was asked of them — so in a diverse federation, 'one person, one vote' constantly collides with 'don't let the biggest states swallow the smaller ones'. That tension is the permanent balancing act of any large democracy.

stories.title: India's first private rocket goes for orbit: Skyroot sets July 18 for the maiden launch of Vikram-1 from Sriharikota
key_points: Skyroot to launch Vikram-1 on July 18 at 11:30 a.m. from Sriharikota
Would be India's first privately built rocket to reach orbit
7-storey rocket, 3D-printed engines; carries up to 350 kg to low orbit
Built by ex-ISRO engineers — a test of India's new private space industry
stories.background: For decades, India's space programme was a government monopoly run by ISRO, the national space agency. In 2020 the government opened the sector to private companies and set up a regulator, IN-SPACe, to license them — aiming to grow a much bigger commercial space economy. Skyroot Aerospace, founded in 2018 by former ISRO engineers, became the first Indian private firm to send up a rocket in 2022, but that was a small 'sub-orbital' test — a hop that goes up and falls back down, not a full flight to orbit.
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, Skyroot announced it will attempt to launch Vikram-1 on July 18 at 11:30 a.m. IST from the first launch pad at the Satish Dhawan Space Centre in Sriharikota. Named Mission Aagaman ('arrival'), the seven-storey, multi-stage rocket — built with an all-carbon-composite body and in-house engines, some of them 3D-printed — is designed to carry small satellites weighing up to 350 kg into low Earth orbit (roughly 450 km up). The company says all stages are already stacked on the pad and final pre-launch checks are complete.
stories.why_it_matters: If Vikram-1 reaches orbit, it would be the first rocket built by a private Indian company ever to do so — clearing the single hardest technical gate in the launch business and proving India's young commercial space industry can compete. It signals India is following the path the US took with firms like SpaceX, where private rockets made launches cheaper and more frequent, and it strengthens India's bid for a bigger share of the fast-growing global market for launching small satellites.
stories.ripple_effects: A success would validate India's 2020 decision to open space to private players and could unlock more funding for Skyroot and rivals like Agnikul, while drawing satellite customers who want cheaper, on-demand rides to orbit. It would also ease ISRO's workload, letting the national agency focus on flagship missions while private firms handle routine small launches. A failure — common in maiden rocket flights — would be a setback but not unusual: even SpaceX's first three orbital attempts blew up before it succeeded.
stories.why_now: The date follows years of engine testing and Skyroot's successful 2022 sub-orbital flight; with all stages now stacked and checks done, the company locked in the July 18 window — riding a government push to build a roughly $44bn Indian space economy by 2033.
stories.watch_next: Whether the July 18 launch actually lifts off on schedule — weather and last-minute checks often force delays — and, crucially, whether Vikram-1 successfully reaches orbit.

key_terms.definition: A rocket powerful enough to reach the speed and height needed to put a satellite into orbit around Earth — far harder than a 'sub-orbital' hop that goes up and comes straight back down.
The band of space roughly 200–2,000 km above Earth where most small satellites — and the International Space Station — circle the planet.
India's space regulator, created in 2020 to open the sector, long an ISRO monopoly, to private companies.

stories.the_lesson: Space is shifting from a government-only endeavour into a private industry, and the first orbital launch is the gate a private space sector must clear before it can compete. When a country lets startups build rockets, launches tend to get cheaper and more frequent — the same shift SpaceX drove in the US, now playing out in India.
stories.market_link: Skyroot is a privately held startup, so there's no direct listed stock to trade — but a successful orbital launch would lift sentiment across India's space-and-defence supply chain and the broader 'private space' investment theme.

stories.title: India's inflation jumps past the RBI's target for the first time in 18 months — pricier fuel and food push June prices up 4.38%
key_points: June retail inflation rose to 4.38%, up from 3.93% in May
First time above the RBI's 4% target since January 2025
Costlier petrol, diesel and food did most of the damage
A patchy monsoon threatens to push food prices higher still
stories.background: 'Inflation' is the pace at which prices rise across the economy — measured in India by the Consumer Price Index (CPI), a fixed basket of everyday goods and services (food, fuel, rent, clothes) whose total cost is tracked each month. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI), the country's central bank, is legally tasked with keeping CPI inflation at 4%, within a 2–6% band. When inflation runs hot, the RBI tends to raise its main interest rate (the 'repo rate') to cool spending; when it's soft, it can cut rates to boost growth. For much of the past year inflation sat comfortably below 4%, giving the RBI room to support the economy.
stories.what_happened: Government data released on July 13, 2026 showed retail inflation accelerated to 4.38% in June, up from 3.93% in May — breaching the RBI's 4% target for the first time since January 2025 and hitting an 18-month high. It also topped forecasts (economists polled had expected about 4.2%). Food inflation climbed to 5.32%, and the transport category jumped to 4.31% from 1.75% — June was the first full month to reflect the mid-May hikes in petrol and diesel prices. Rural inflation (4.74%) ran hotter than urban (3.92%).
stories.why_it_matters: Crossing the 4% target is a signal the RBI cannot ignore, because its whole mandate is anchored to that number. Higher inflation eats into what every household's money can buy — the same salary stretches less far — and it narrows the central bank's room to cut interest rates to support growth. For a reader in India, this is the number that quietly shapes loan EMIs, savings returns, and the price of the weekly grocery run.
stories.ripple_effects: With inflation above target, the RBI is less likely to cut interest rates soon — which keeps home, car and business loan rates elevated and can weigh on stocks that thrive on cheap money. It squeezes household budgets, especially in rural areas and for lower-income families who spend a bigger share of their income on food. A weaker rupee and pricey imported oil — worsened by the US-Iran conflict pushing up crude — feed straight into fuel and transport costs, risking a second-round rise. And a patchy monsoon, which waters most of India's crops, could lift food prices further in July, keeping the pressure on.
stories.why_now: Two forces collided in June: the mid-May increase in petrol and diesel prices finally showed up in a full month of data, and food prices firmed as an uneven monsoon raised worries about the harvest — together tipping inflation over the 4% line just as global oil stayed jittery from the Middle East war.
stories.watch_next: The RBI's next interest-rate decision on August 5, 2026 — most economists expect it to hold rates steady rather than hike, betting that cooling global oil prices will pull inflation back down.

key_terms.definition: A monthly gauge of the average price of a fixed basket of everyday goods and services; the main measure of India's retail inflation.
The interest rate at which the RBI lends to banks — its main lever to control inflation; raising it makes borrowing costlier and cools demand.
The RBI is mandated to keep CPI inflation at 4%, within a 2–6% tolerance band.
India's June–September rainy season that waters most of the country's crops; a weak or uneven monsoon can push food prices up.

stories.the_lesson: A central bank's interest-rate decisions swing on one number — inflation versus its target — so when prices cross that line, borrowing costs and your loan EMIs are the next thing in play. And inflation is often imported: a war thousands of miles away can raise the price of the fuel in your tank and the food on your plate.
stories.market_link: Sticky-above-target inflation trims hopes of near-term RBI rate cuts — a headwind for rate-sensitive Indian stocks (banks, autos, real estate) and for bonds; the rupee and oil-import costs are the key swing factors to watch.

stories.title: India vs England ODI series goes to a Lord's decider — with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight
key_points: Series level 1-1 after England win in Cardiff
Decider at Lord's; India seek first series win in England since 2018
Rohit Sharma under retirement speculation, joins optional practice
Harsh Dubey replaces injured Washington Sundar
stories.background: India and England are playing a three-match one-day international (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. Veteran opener Rohit Sharma, 39, was recently removed as India's ODI captain and has struggled for runs, fuelling talk that this could be one of his last outings and that younger opener Yashasvi Jaiswal is ready to replace him.
stories.what_happened: England levelled the series 1-1 by winning the second ODI in Cardiff, guided by Joe Root's unbeaten 99. That sets up a winner-takes-all third ODI at Lord's, cricket's most famous ground. Amid swirling retirement rumours, Rohit turned up for India's optional practice and looked in good touch, while India named all-rounder Harsh Dubey to replace the injured Washington Sundar, opening the door for spinner Kuldeep Yadav's possible return. India have not won a bilateral ODI series in England since 2018.
stories.why_it_matters: It's a genuine decider with a personal subplot: a legend of Indian cricket may be playing to prove he still belongs, in the same match that decides the series. For India, ending a long drought of ODI series wins in England would be a marker of the team's transition to a younger core.
stories.ripple_effects: Rohit's performance could shape selection debates and his own career timeline heading toward major tournaments; a series win or loss feeds into how India rebuilds its one-day side. For England, backing up their Cardiff comeback would validate a reshaped ODI unit.
stories.why_now: The Cardiff result squared the series at 1-1, so everything rests on the final match at Lord's this weekend.
stories.watch_next: The result of the Lord's decider — and whether Rohit's showing quiets or amplifies the retirement talk.

key_terms.definition: A limited-overs cricket match where each side bats for 50 overs, usually finished in a single day.
A series played between just two nations, separate from multi-team tournaments like the World Cup.

stories.the_lesson: In sport, individual and team stories often collide in one game — pressure on an ageing star can either sharpen a career-defining performance or hasten its end.

stories.title: Antonelli beats Verstappen to pole at the Belgian GP as Mercedes keeps dominating qualifying
key_points: Kimi Antonelli takes his 6th pole of 2026
Beat Max Verstappen by 0.317s at Spa
Verstappen aided by a team-mate's slipstream
Champion Lando Norris hit with a 10-place grid penalty
stories.background: Formula 1's qualifying decides the starting order for Sunday's race, and starting up front is a big advantage on tracks where overtaking is hard. Italian teenager Kimi Antonelli, in only his second F1 season with Mercedes, leads the 2026 drivers' championship, and Mercedes cars have been fastest over one lap all year.
stories.what_happened: At the Belgian Grand Prix at Spa-Francorchamps, Antonelli clinched his sixth pole position of the season, edging four-time champion Max Verstappen by 0.317 seconds. Verstappen admitted he only reached the front row thanks to a 'tow' — a slipstream — from Red Bull team-mate Isack Hadjar, who runs at the back after engine-change penalties. Reigning champion Lando Norris will drop 10 places on the grid for using too many engine parts, and Lewis Hamilton crashed his Ferrari in final practice earlier in the day.
stories.why_it_matters: It reinforces Antonelli as the man to beat in a breakout season and tightens Mercedes' grip on qualifying. Grid penalties for Norris and others reshuffle the order, giving Antonelli a strong chance to convert pole into points and extend his title lead.
stories.ripple_effects: Track position at Spa can decide the race, so this hands Antonelli momentum in the championship; Norris's penalty forces a recovery drive; and Verstappen's reliance on a team-mate's tow hints at Red Bull's pace deficit. Rivals will study Mercedes' one-lap edge before the next round.
stories.why_now: Saturday qualifying set the grid for Sunday's Belgian Grand Prix.
stories.watch_next: Sunday's race — whether Antonelli converts pole into a win, and how far Norris climbs from his penalty.

key_terms.definition: The first place on the starting grid, earned by the fastest lap in qualifying.
Following closely behind another car to sit in its wake, cutting air resistance and gaining speed.

stories.the_lesson: Even in a sport built on individual speed, small tactics like slipstreaming and the rules on engine parts can decide who starts where — proving strategy and reliability matter as much as raw pace.

disclaimer: Decoded from public reporting for general understanding. Verify before relying on any single claim; details evolve.
